title: Operating Systems
prerequisites: Students must have completed both CS201 and CS202 before enrolling
description: Study of operating system principles including process management, memory management, file systems, and I/O management. Students learn about process scheduling algorithms, inter-process communication (IPC), synchronization primitives (mutexes, semaphores, monitors), deadlock detection and prevention, virtual memory with paging and segmentation, and file system implementations. The course includes substantial systems programming assignments in C, requiring students to implement simplified versions of OS components. Students must have completed both CS201 (for data structures used in OS internals) and CS202 (for mathematical foundations of scheduling analysis). A concurrent co-requisite lab section CS303L provides hands-on experience with Linux kernel programming. Note: This course is typically offered only in the Fall semester.
topics: Virtual memory and page replacement algorithms
outcomes: Explain and compare process scheduling algorithms
Implement concurrent programs with proper synchronization
Explain virtual memory mechanisms and page replacement policies
Understand file system organization and implementation
Write systems-level programs in C using POSIX APIs
